steps.title: Gastroenterology and food allergy
steps.text: Multiple Choice for Primary Care

steps.title: Chronic Abdominal Pain
steps.text: What is an appropriate initial step in the management of chronic abdominal pain in a child at the primary care level when no red flags are present?
steps.rationale: In the absence of red flags, deworming is a recommended first-line intervention in primary care for chronic abdominal pain.

steps.title: Chronic Abdominal Pain
steps.text: Which of the following is a red flag that warrants referral of a child with chronic abdominal pain from primary care?
steps.rationale: Gastrointestinal bleeding is a significant red flag and requires referral to secondary or tertiary care for further evaluation and management.

textChoices.text: Mild abdominal discomfort after large meals
Gastrointestinal blood loss (e.g. bloody diarrhoea or melena)

steps.title: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease
steps.text: A healthy infant has frequent regurgitation but is feeding well and gaining weight. What should be your first step?
steps.rationale: Most infants with uncomplicated regurgitation need reassurance and monitoring, not medication.

steps.title: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease
steps.text: Which of the following should be advised for a school-age child with mild GERD symptoms?
steps.rationale: Avoiding large meals and waiting before lying down reduces reflux in older children.

textChoices.text: Avoid large meals and late eating

steps.title: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease
steps.text: What helps reduce regurgitation in an infant with GER?
steps.rationale: Thickening feeds and upright positioning after feeding are effective non-pharmacological strategies.

textChoices.text: Add rice cereal and keep upright

steps.title: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease
steps.text: When should you refer a child with suspected GERD?
steps.rationale: Referral is needed when conservative management fails or red flags are present.

textChoices.text: At the first sign of spit-up
If symptoms don’t improve with basic care
If reflux occurs less than once a week
At the first episode of vomiting

steps.title: Peptic Ulcer Disease (PUD)
steps.text: What is an appropriate first step in managing a child suspected of having peptic ulcer disease (PUD) at the primary care level?
steps.rationale: Primary management includes stopping aggravating medications like NSAIDs and giving antacids, while preparing for referral.

textChoices.text: Start long-term PPIs and observe at home
Prescribe antibiotics and refer in 2 weeks

steps.title: Chronic Diarrhea
steps.text: What is the most appropriate management of a child presenting with chronic diarrhea at a primary health facility?
steps.rationale: At the primary care level, the priority is to correct dehydration and refer the child for further investigation and management. Anti-diarrheals are not appropriate in young children.

textChoices.text: Correct dehydration and refer to higher level of care

steps.title: Constipation
steps.text: Which of the following is an effective preventive measure for constipation in children?
steps.rationale: Preventive care includes a high-fiber diet, hydration, and routine toilet habits to reduce constipation risk.

textChoices.text: Limiting fiber and increasing dairy intake
Encouraging processed snacks and low-residue diet
High-fiber diet, increased fluid intake, and regular toilet routine

steps.title: Constipation
steps.text: Which of the following findings should prompt referral of a constipated child to secondary or tertiary care?
steps.rationale: Delayed meconium passage may suggest conditions like Hirschsprung’s disease or cystic fibrosis and requires specialist evaluation.

textChoices.text: Delayed passage of meconium beyond 48 hours after birth
Constipation for 3 days without other symptoms
Dietary history showing low vegetable intake

steps.title: Constipation
steps.text: Which of the following is a red flag in a child with constipation that warrants referral?
steps.rationale: Abdominal distension and failure to thrive may point to serious underlying conditions like Hirschsprung’s disease or malabsorption, requiring specialist referral.

textChoices.text: Preference for milk over solid food
Abdominal distension and failure to thrive

steps.title: Food allergy
steps.text: A child presents with sudden wheezing, rash, and hypotension after eating peanuts. What is your first priority?
steps.rationale: A child with signs of anaphylaxis requires immediate stabilization using the ABCDE approach and emergency management protocols.

textChoices.text: Initiate ABCDE and manage as anaphylaxis
Give antihistamines and monitor at home
Ask the family to remove peanuts from diet and return if worse

steps.title: Food allergy
steps.text: What is the next best step in a child with recurrent hives after certain meals, but currently stable?
steps.rationale: A thorough history is essential, but all suspected food allergy cases should be referred to higher-level care for further evaluation and testing.

textChoices.text: Take detailed dietary history and refer
Perform skin prick test in primary care
Start an elimination diet without documentation

steps.title: Inflammatory Bowel Disease
steps.text: Which of the following symptom combinations should raise suspicion for IBD in a child?
steps.rationale: Chronic diarrhea, rectal bleeding, and growth retardation are classic features of inflammatory bowel disease, especially Crohn’s disease.

textChoices.text: Fever and isolated upper abdominal pain after meals
Chronic diarrhea with rectal bleeding and growth retardation

steps.title: Inflammatory Bowel Disease
steps.text: Which of the following symptoms is more commonly associated with Crohn’s disease than ulcerative colitis in children?
steps.rationale: Crohn’s disease is more often associated with aphthous ulcers and growth retardation, which may precede GI symptoms and help differentiate it from ulcerative colitis.

textChoices.text: Aphthous oral ulcers and growth failure

steps.title: Coeliac disease
steps.text: A 2-year-old presents with chronic diarrhea, abdominal distension, irritability, and failure to thrive. What is the most appropriate step at a primary care facility?
steps.rationale: At the primary level, children with symptoms suggestive of coeliac disease should be stabilized (e.g. for dehydration) and referred for confirmatory testing and specialist management.

textChoices.text: Prescribe iron supplements and schedule follow-up
Correct dehydration and refer to higher level for further evaluation

steps.title: Acute Liver Failure
steps.text: A 6-year-old presents with jaundice, confusion, and a history of vomiting. Blood sugar is 1.8 mmol/L. What is the most appropriate immediate action at the primary care level?
steps.rationale: At the primary level, management of acute liver failure involves emergency stabilization (ABCDE, glucose correction, fluid bolus) and urgent referral to a higher-level facility for definitive care.

textChoices.text: Start oral rehydration and monitor for 24 hours
Begin ABCDE stabilization, treat hypoglycaemia, and refer
Wait for liver function test results before acting

steps.title: Liver Cirrhosis
steps.text: A 10-year-old with suspected liver cirrhosis presents with confusion and vomiting. What is the most appropriate initial step at a primary or secondary care facility?
steps.rationale: Stabilization and checking for hypoglycemia are key priorities in any child presenting with signs of hepatic decompensation.

textChoices.text: Stabilize the patient and check blood sugar

steps.title: Liver Cirrhosis
steps.text: What is the recommended first-line treatment for coagulopathy-related bleeding in a child with liver cirrhosis?
steps.rationale: Vitamin K (2.5–10 mg/day) is used to manage bleeding due to impaired synthesis of clotting factors in chronic liver disease.

steps.title: Liver Cirrhosis
steps.text: Which of the following actions is appropriate in the care of a child with chronic liver disease at the primary or secondary level?
steps.rationale: Mid-upper arm circumference is useful for nutritional assessment, and palliative care involvement is crucial for chronic or end-stage cases.

textChoices.text: Perform nutritional assessment and refer to palliative care if needed
Delay referral until liver enzymes normalize

steps.title: Portal hypertension
steps.text: A 6-year-old presents with splenomegaly, abdominal distension, and hematemesis. You suspect portal hypertension. What is the most appropriate action at a primary or secondary care facility?
steps.rationale: At the primary or secondary level, the priority is stabilization (manage airway, breathing, circulation) and urgent referral to tertiary care for further diagnostic workup and definitive management of portal hypertension.

textChoices.text: Stabilize the patient and refer to tertiary care
Order a liver biopsy before referring

steps.title: Ascites
steps.text: A 5-year-old presents to a primary care clinic with visible abdominal distension and difficulty breathing. Ascites is suspected. What is the most appropriate next step?
steps.rationale: At the primary level, the correct approach to suspected ascites is to stabilize the child and refer to secondary/tertiary care for definitive diagnosis and management. Invasive procedures like paracentesis are not performed at this level.

textChoices.text: Prescribe oral antibiotics and review in one week
Stabilize the patient and refer to higher-level care
Perform abdominal paracentesis in the clinic
